items: Single-case study without prospective implementation; generalizability and robustness across pathogens, gene families, and sample types remain untested.
Reliance on a largely black-box resistance prediction (EPI2ME CARD protein homolog model) with an “accuracy >90%” threshold that is not rigorously defined or justified.

The “copy number” (CN) reported in Table 1 appears to reflect read/alignments counts rather than true genomic copy number; plasmid copy number and gene dosage are not quantitatively estimated using normalized coverage.

ONT-only assemblies (R9.4.1, HAC, three rounds of Racon) may be insufficient to confidently call novel β-lactamase variants without additional polishing (e.g., Medaka, Illumina hybrid) or orthogonal confirmation.
No orthogonal wet-lab validation (e.g., targeted PCR/qPCR/Sanger) of the low-abundance KPC-14/159 in the pre-treatment isolate.
Absence of negative controls and contamination controls for library prep and sequencing; multiple CFUs used for DNA extraction may obscure within-sample population structure.
No explicit decision threshold or confidence framework for when to “call” actionable resistance in real time; “R?” in Table 1 is underspecified.
Lack of rigorous quantification of time-to-result (including compute times) and cost analysis relative to standard diagnostics.
The use of “accuracy” in EPI2ME outputs and the meaning of “CN” require precise definitions; the current presentation risks over-interpretation.
The lineage is reported as ST1472; it is unclear if this denotes ST147 or a sublineage—needs clarification and cgMLST/SNP distance data to support “same lineage.”
Harmonization between KPC-14 (bioinformatic label) and KPC-159 (final classification) is needed in the main text, with explicit variant coordinates and functional rationale.
Limited benchmarking against other resistome tools (e.g., ResFinder, Abricate/ABRicate, ARIBA, Mykrobe) and nanopore-specific callers or polishing workflows.
Broader clinical metagenomics validation literature and discussion of limitations (e.g., false discovery control for low-abundance hits) could be better integrated.
Potential confounding by age and sex across diagnostic groups (e.g., LNB older than viral meningitis), without clear covariate adjustment in DE analyses or ML models.
Batch effects, sample handling, and instrument drift are not sufficiently detailed; unclear if batch correction/bridging and QC standards were employed.

Limited reporting of statistical procedures (criteria for “significant,” multiple-testing correction, imputation details, normalization), making reproducibility and inferential strength harder to judge.
The plasma classifier’s validation AUC of 0.80 is promising but modest for clinical decision-making; panel stability across cohorts appears lower than in CSF.

Control group definitions and inclusion/exclusion criteria are not fully described; lack of disease controls beyond viral meningitis (e.g., autoimmune/inflammatory neurologic conditions, bacterial meningitis, other neuropathies) limits specificity assessment.
No formal comparison against, or augmentation over, standard-of-care diagnostics (e.g., pleocytosis, intrathecal antibody index, CXCL13 ELISA), nor analysis of incremental value.

Early-disease LNB cases (with negative or borderline intrathecal antibody index) are not evaluated; current selection (first-time positive index) may enrich for established immune responses and overestimate performance for early diagnosis.
Plasma analyses do not attempt LNB vs viral meningitis classification, despite having some VM samples, limiting clinical utility in blood-only scenarios.
Claims of “turnaround time of just 2 hours” are insufficiently substantiated with end-to-end workflow details (prep, acquisition, analysis).
Some key methodological specifics (hyperparameter tuning, nested CV, feature selection protocol, leakage prevention) are not fully described.
Limited discussion of how the approach compares to targeted proteomics/ELISA panels (e.g., CXCL13) in sensitivity/specificity, cost, and scalability.
No benchmarking against simple clinical models (e.g., age, CRP, CSF WBC) to quantify added value of MS proteomics.

Mechanistic claims (e.g., role of Tyr211 and loop flexibility in disulfide-state switching) are plausibly argued but not directly validated biochemically (e.g., disulfide-state assays, crosslinking, or protease sensitivity measurements).
TcpP/TcpH dependence on E. coli RseP and stoichiometry is inferred by function; direct tests of proteolysis and stability are limited.
The LacZ assay relies on cell lysis and added detergents; robustness against variable sample matrices and potential inhibitors in serum is not fully explored.
Clinical validation cohort is small (n=21), heterogeneous, and not blinded; limited power to assess sensitivity/specificity and clinical decision thresholds.
Lack of spike-and-recovery experiments in serum to quantify matrix effects on LOD, dynamic range, and specificity.
Stability, shelf-life, and performance under field conditions (temperature, lyophilization, storage) are not assessed.
No explicit evaluation of interfering substances (e.g., hemolysis, lipemia, common medications, detergents, antibiotics, immunosuppressants) that may be present in transplant patient sera.
Transformation/library coverage details are not fully quantified; actual diversity explored post-selection remains unclear beyond logos and selected variants.
Some figure references and supplementary cross-links (e.g., kinetics, gating strategies) could be more explicitly summarized in the main text to aid readers.
The claim that the platform is “portable” would benefit from explicit operational parameters (time-to-result, need for incubation hardware, cold chain, assay footprint).
Limited discussion contrasting performance and costs with current enzymatic kits and LC-MS profiling (sensitivity, specificity, quantitation range).
Additional context on other synthetic receptor scaffolds and recent whole-cell or cell-free bile acid assays would strengthen positioning.

Evidence for a gel-like (liquid–solid) state is largely indirect (slow exchange and qualitative fusion behavior); lacks quantitative mobility assays (e.g., FRAP/FRAP-recovery profiles), microrheology, or sensitivity to solvents (e.g., 1,6-hexanediol) that would more cleanly differentiate gels vs. liquids.

High salt (750 mM NaCl) and 10% PEG crowding in vitro may bias toward nonspecific condensation; broader buffer mapping (salt/PEG/Mg2+ dependence) is needed to test specificity and physiological relevance.
The inference that burst steps correspond to RNA–protein complex units depends on fluorescence linearity, labeling fraction, and photophysics; calibration is not fully established.
Lack of FRAP or single-molecule tracking to directly quantify internal rearrangement and percolation thresholds; no rheological or relaxation spectrum measurements.
The SIM-based shell/core interpretation is qualitative; no radial intensity quantification, 3D z-stacks, or co-localization statistics are provided.

The “protein capacitor” claim (increased cellular protein levels) lacks mechanistic disentanglement from altered expression, maturation, or proteolysis; no measurements of protein turnover, mRNA levels, or growth burden.
In vitro nonlinearity and the “K0” parameter are discussed but not adequately defined or cross-validated (e.g., with lifetime/anisotropy to confirm quenching).
The modified Poisson fit and the physical meaning of fit parameters (λ, K0) are insufficiently explained; it is unclear how these map to molecular counts vs. network topology.
Step-detection statistics lack a comprehensive description of the noise model, bleaching correction, and robustness across acquisition settings.
Limited discussion of how results align with multivalency-controlled phase diagrams and percolation/gelation theory (e.g., lattice mean-field multivalency models).

Under-discusses prior observations that MS2/PP7 coat proteins with long RNAs can yield condensates at high protein:RNA ratios and how this relates to their finding that PP7 promotes condensation even when RNA alone does not.
The role of RNA in tuning viscoelasticity seen in protein–RNA condensate literature is not fully connected to their class I vs class II permeability observations.
